description: Primary key for asset_sampling_point

business_glossary_term: Asset Sampling Point - Sampling Point Id
description: Foreign key linking to the approved water quality sampling location where samples are collected

business_glossary_term: Reference to single source of truth in quality.quality_sampling_point
description: Foreign key to the canonical quality_sampling_point record in quality domain

business_glossary_term: Asset Sampling Point - Asset Registry Id
description: Foreign key linking to the physical infrastructure asset being monitored (tank, pump station, treatment unit, pipe segment, valve, etc.)
Current operational status of this asset-sampling point configuration. Active = in use; suspended = temporarily inactive; under_review = pending validation; decommissioned = permanently retired.
Date when this asset-sampling point configuration was last reviewed and validated as part of monitoring plan audits or regulatory compliance reviews.
Indicates whether sampling at this specific asset-sampling point combination is mandated by regulatory permit conditions (NPDES, SDWA, state requirements) versus operational/voluntary monitoring.
Date when this asset-sampling point configuration was deactivated (e.g., asset decommissioned, sampling point relocated, monitoring requirements changed). Null if currently active.
Date when this asset-sampling point configuration became active and sampling began for this specific asset at this location. Used to track historical changes in monitoring configurations.

Identifies which team or entity is responsible for collecting samples at this asset-sampling point combination (may vary by asset even at the same sampling location based on access requirements or operational constraints).

Asset-specific access instructions, safety requirements, or logistical notes for sampling personnel collecting samples at this location for this particular asset (e.g., 'Requires confined space entry permit for tank interior sampling', 'Access via north valve vault only', 'Coordinate with operations for pump shutdown').

Asset-specific override of the default sampling frequency for this sampling point. Allows different sampling cadences for different assets monitored at the same location (e.g., critical assets sampled daily while standard assets sampled weekly at the same point).

Priority ranking (1=highest) for this asset-sampling point combination used to sequence sampling activities when multiple assets are monitored at the same location. Based on asset criticality, regulatory requirements, and operational risk.
Unique surrogate primary key for each asset-requirement compliance relationship record

business_glossary_term: Asset Compliance Requirement - Asset Registry Id
description: Foreign key linking to the physical infrastructure asset subject to the regulatory requirement

business_glossary_term: Asset Compliance Requirement - Regulatory Requirement Id
description: Foreign key linking to the regulatory requirement that applies to the asset

The specific method, control, or operational practice by which this asset achieves compliance with this requirement (e.g., 'continuous monitoring', 'quarterly sampling', 'treatment technique', 'operational control'). Varies by asset-requirement combination.
Free-text notes documenting compliance issues, corrective actions, assessment findings, or special considerations specific to this asset-requirement relationship.

Current compliance status of this specific asset with respect to this specific regulatory requirement. Tracks whether the asset meets the requirement, is out of compliance, is pending assessment, or has been granted a variance.
Timestamp when this asset-requirement compliance relationship record was created in the system.

Date on which this regulatory requirement became applicable to this specific asset. May differ from the requirement's general effective date based on asset installation date, facility type, or system size.
Date of the most recent compliance assessment performed for this asset against this specific regulatory requirement. Used to track assessment currency and trigger next assessment scheduling.
Scheduled date for the next compliance assessment of this asset against this regulatory requirement. Calculated based on the requirement's monitoring frequency and the last assessment date.

Name or identifier of the individual responsible for ensuring this specific asset maintains compliance with this specific requirement. May differ from the general requirement owner based on asset location or operational area.
Timestamp of the most recent update to this asset-requirement compliance relationship record.
Expiration date of the granted variance or exemption for this asset-requirement pair. Null if no variance is granted or if variance is permanent.
Indicates whether a regulatory variance, exemption, or waiver has been granted for this specific asset-requirement combination. True if variance is active, false otherwise.
Unique system-generated identifier for the depreciation schedule record. Primary key for this entity.

Foreign key linking to project.cip_project. Business justification: Capitalized assets from CIP projects require depreciation schedules linked to originating project for GASB 34 compliance, rate case justification, and audit trail connecting capital expenditure author
Reference to the capital asset for which this depreciation schedule is maintained. Links to the enterprise asset registry.
Reference to the capital asset for which this depreciation schedule is maintained. Links to the enterprise asset registry.

Foreign key linking to finance.fixed_asset. Business justification: Depreciation schedules calculate periodic depreciation expense for fixed assets. Direct link supports automated depreciation posting, GASB 34 infrastructure reporting, and rate base calculations for u
Total depreciation expense recognized from the depreciation start date through the end of the current fiscal period. Represents the contra-asset balance on the balance sheet. Expressed in USD.

Total capitalized cost of the asset at acquisition, including purchase price, installation, freight, and any directly attributable costs required to bring the asset to its intended use. Expressed in USD.
The total depreciation expense allocated for the full fiscal year for this asset. For straight-line method: depreciable_base / useful_life_years. Expressed in USD.
SAP FI-AA asset class code categorizing the type of capital asset for depreciation area assignment and useful life defaults (e.g., 'PUMP', 'PIPE', 'BLDG', 'VEHICLE', 'ELEC', 'METER').
Human-readable description of the capital asset being depreciated (e.g., 'WTP High-Service Pump Unit 3', 'WWTP Aeration Blower', '12-inch Ductile Iron Main - Oak Street').

Functional location or facility code where the asset is installed (e.g., WTP facility code, WWTP unit, pump station ID, distribution zone). Aligns with IBM Maximo functional location hierarchy and Esri ArcGIS GIS asset registry.
The externally-known fixed asset number as assigned in SAP FI/CO Fixed Asset Accounting (FI-AA) or IBM Maximo. Used for cross-system reconciliation and audit trails.
The Capital Improvement Program (CIP) project number associated with the asset's acquisition or construction. Links depreciation schedules to CAPEX planning and project close-out reporting.

The date the asset was formally capitalized and transferred from construction-in-progress (CIP) to fixed assets in SAP FI-AA. May differ from depreciation start date for assets with a commissioning lag.
SAP FI company code representing the legal entity or municipal utility enterprise to which this depreciation schedule belongs. Supports multi-entity financial reporting and GASB fund accounting.
SAP CO cost center to which depreciation charges are posted, enabling OPEX/CAPEX allocation by operational unit (e.g., WTP operations, distribution, wastewater).
The percentage rate applied per period for declining balance or double declining balance depreciation methods. Expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.2000 = 20%). Null for straight-line method.
The amount subject to depreciation, calculated as acquisition cost minus salvage value. This is the total amount to be allocated over the asset's useful life. Expressed in USD.

SAP FI-AA depreciation area identifier representing the valuation purpose (e.g., '01' for book depreciation per GAAP/GASB, '15' for tax depreciation, '20' for cost accounting). Supports parallel valuation for regulatory and tax reporting.
The period convention applied to determine depreciation in the first and last year of the asset's life (e.g., full_month, half_year, pro_rata). Aligns with SAP FI-AA period control settings.
The projected or actual date on which depreciation ceases, corresponding to the end of the asset's useful life or full depreciation of the depreciable base.

SAP FI-AA depreciation key code that defines the calculation method, period control, and base value for depreciation posting (e.g., 'LINR' for straight-line, '0000' for no depreciation). Enables direct reconciliation with SAP Fixed Asset Accounting.

Accounting method used to allocate the depreciable base over the asset's useful life. Straight-line is most common for municipal water utility infrastructure per GASB. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: straight_line|declining_balance|double_declining_balance|units_of_production|sum_of_years_digits — promote to reference product]
The date on which depreciation begins for the asset, typically the in-service date or acquisition date per GAAP/GASB capitalization policy.
The date the asset was retired, disposed of, or decommissioned. Triggers cessation of depreciation and recognition of any gain or loss on disposal in SAP FI-AA.
Cash or fair value received upon disposal or sale of the asset. Used to calculate gain or loss on disposal: disposal_proceeds minus net_book_value at disposal date. Expressed in USD.
The accounting period (month 1–12) within the fiscal year for which this depreciation record applies. Enables monthly depreciation posting and period-end close reconciliation in SAP FI/CO.
The fiscal year to which this depreciation schedule record applies. Supports annual depreciation posting, CAPEX/OPEX reporting, and GASB financial statement preparation.
Governmental fund code (e.g., Enterprise Fund, Capital Projects Fund) to which the asset and its depreciation are attributed, per GASB fund accounting requirements for municipal utilities.

SAP FI General Ledger account code for the depreciation expense posting (e.g., the depreciation expense account and accumulated depreciation contra-asset account). Enables financial statement mapping.

Cumulative impairment charges recognized against the asset when its carrying value exceeds its recoverable amount, per GAAP ASC 360 impairment testing. Reduces net book value beyond scheduled depreciation. Expressed in USD.
The date the asset was placed into operational service. This is the trigger date for depreciation commencement per GAAP/GASB policy and aligns with IBM Maximo asset activation.
Date the depreciation schedule was last reviewed and validated by the asset accounting team, supporting annual audit requirements and GASB financial statement preparation.

Current carrying value of the asset on the balance sheet, calculated as acquisition cost minus accumulated depreciation. Key metric for CAPEX/OPEX planning and asset renewal decisions. Expressed in USD.

Free-text field for recording significant accounting judgments, useful life revision rationale, impairment triggers, regulatory directives, or other material information relevant to this depreciation schedule.
The depreciation expense allocated for the specific fiscal period (month). Used for monthly journal entry posting to the depreciation expense account in SAP FI/CO. Expressed in USD.
The timestamp when this depreciation schedule record was first created in the system, supporting audit trail and data lineage requirements.
The timestamp when this depreciation schedule record was last modified, supporting change tracking, audit trail, and period-end reconciliation.
Estimated remaining useful life of the asset in years as of the last assessment date. Used for condition-based depreciation adjustments and asset renewal planning under CIP.
Name or employee ID of the asset accountant or finance officer who last reviewed and approved this depreciation schedule record, supporting audit trail and SOX/GASB compliance.
Estimated residual value of the asset at the end of its useful life, used to calculate the depreciable base. For most water utility infrastructure, this is often zero. Expressed in USD.

Current lifecycle status of the depreciation schedule record. 'active' = depreciation ongoing; 'suspended' = temporarily halted (e.g., asset taken out of service); 'fully_depreciated' = net book value at or near salvage value; 'disposed' = asset retired; 'impaired' = impairment recognized; 'draft' = pending approval.

Actual production units consumed during the current fiscal period, used to calculate period depreciation under the units-of-production method (e.g., gallons pumped, operating hours logged via SCADA/OSIsoft PI Historian).

Total estimated production units over the asset's useful life, used as the denominator for units-of-production depreciation method (e.g., total gallons pumped, total operating hours). Applicable only when depreciation_method = 'units_of_production'.
Updated estimated useful life in years following a formal engineering reassessment or condition assessment. Replaces original useful_life_years for prospective depreciation calculation.
Date on which the asset's estimated useful life was last revised, triggering a prospective change in depreciation calculation per GAAP ASC 250 change in accounting estimate.

Estimated useful life of the asset in years, as determined by engineering assessment and aligned with AWWA asset life guidelines and GASB/GAAP standards (e.g., 50 years for water mains, 20 years for pumps, 10 years for meters).
SAP PS Work Breakdown Structure element linking the asset to its originating Capital Improvement Program (CIP) project for CAPEX tracking and project settlement.
Unique identifier for this asset-grant funding relationship record. Primary key.
Foreign key linking to the grant providing funding for the asset

business_glossary_term: Asset Grant Funding - Asset Registry Id
description: Foreign key linking to the physical infrastructure asset being funded by the grant
Dollar amount of grant funds allocated to this specific asset from this specific grant. Used for reimbursement tracking and audit reporting.
Cumulative dollar amount that has been reimbursed by the grantor for this asset under this grant. Used to calculate remaining funding balance.

Current compliance status of this asset-grant funding relationship with respect to grantor requirements. Values: compliant (meets all conditions), non_compliant (violation identified), under_review (audit in progress), pending_documentation (awaiting supporting docs), closed (funding complete and closed out).
Timestamp when this asset-grant funding record was created in the system.
Dollar amount of costs incurred for this asset that have been determined eligible for reimbursement under this grant's allowable cost categories.
Date on which funding for this asset under this grant ends, typically aligned with the grant's period of performance end date or asset completion date.
Date on which this asset became eligible for funding under this grant, typically aligned with the grant's period of performance start date or asset installation date.
Percentage of the asset's total cost that is funded by this grant (e.g., 75.00 means 75% grant-funded, 25% utility match). Used for matching requirement calculations.

Dollar amount of costs incurred for this asset that have been determined ineligible for reimbursement under this grant (e.g., costs incurred outside period of performance, non-allowable cost categories).
Date of the most recent drawdown request submitted to the grantor for reimbursement of costs associated with this asset under this grant.
Timestamp when this asset-grant funding record was last modified.
Free-text notes documenting special conditions, compliance issues, or other relevant information about this asset-grant funding relationship.
Current phase of the capital project for this asset under this grant. Used to track project milestones and align with grant drawdown schedules.

Unique system-generated identifier for each asset warranty record in the enterprise asset management system. Primary key for the asset_warranty data product.

Foreign key linking to project.construction_contract. Business justification: Equipment warranties originate from construction contracts; warranty claim processing, vendor accountability, and performance bond claims require direct reference to the construction contract that spe

Foreign key linking to workforce.employee. Business justification: Warranty record creation audit for procurement process tracking, warranty management program accountability, and data quality monitoring.
Reference to the vendor or manufacturer who issued and is responsible for honoring this warranty. Used to route warranty claims to the correct counterparty.
Reference to the physical infrastructure asset (e.g., pump, valve, pipe, electrical panel, vehicle, WTP/WWTP equipment) covered by this warranty record.
Reference to the physical infrastructure asset (e.g., pump, valve, pipe, electrical panel, vehicle, WTP/WWTP equipment) covered by this warranty record.
Contractually committed number of calendar days within which the vendor must respond to a submitted warranty claim. Used for SLA monitoring and vendor performance management.

Description of the required process for submitting a warranty claim, including required documentation, submission channel (e.g., vendor portal URL, email, phone), and any pre-authorization requirements. Ensures maintenance staff follow correct procedures.
Cumulative monetary value of all warranty claims submitted against this warranty record to date, expressed in USD. Used to track remaining claim capacity against the maximum claim value.

Specific sub-component or assembly of the asset covered by this warranty record (e.g., 'motor winding', 'pump impeller', 'control panel', 'membrane module'). A single asset may have multiple warranty records covering different components.
The vendor-issued or internally-assigned contract number governing the warranty terms and conditions. Used for legal reference and claim dispute resolution.
The date on which warranty coverage expires. Proactive monitoring of this date enables timely claim submission and maintenance routing through warranty channels before expiration, reducing OPEX.

Narrative description of what is covered under this warranty, including specific components, failure modes, and exclusions. For example: 'Covers all mechanical and electrical components of the centrifugal pump excluding wear parts (seals, impellers) and damage caused by cavitation or improper operation.'
The date on which warranty coverage becomes effective. Typically aligned with the asset installation date, commissioning date, or purchase date depending on warranty terms.
The timestamp when this warranty record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail, data lineage, and compliance reporting.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code applicable to all monetary values on this warranty record (e.g., USD). Supports multi-currency environments for international equipment procurement.
Total length of the warranty coverage period expressed in months. Derived from the vendor contract terms and used for planning and renewal tracking.
Explicit list of conditions, components, or failure modes NOT covered by this warranty. Critical for maintenance routing decisions to avoid voiding coverage through excluded repair activities.

Number of calendar days before the coverage end date at which an automated alert should be triggered to notify asset managers and maintenance planners of impending warranty expiration. Enables proactive claim submission and maintenance routing.
The cost paid to purchase an extended warranty beyond the standard manufacturer warranty period, expressed in USD. Captured for CAPEX/OPEX planning and ROI analysis of extended warranty investments.
The date the covered asset or component was physically installed and commissioned. Often used as the warranty coverage start trigger and is required for warranty claim substantiation.
The timestamp when this warranty record was most recently modified. Used for change tracking, audit trail, and incremental data pipeline processing in the Databricks lakehouse silver layer.
The maximum cumulative monetary value of claims that can be submitted under this warranty agreement, expressed in USD. Enables OPEX planning and ensures claims do not exceed contractual limits.

The native warranty record identifier from IBM Maximo Asset Management (CMMS). Retained as a cross-reference key to enable bidirectional traceability between the lakehouse silver layer and the operational CMMS source system.

Free-text field for additional warranty-related information not captured in structured fields, such as special claim conditions, vendor negotiation history, or maintenance routing instructions specific to this warranty.

For performance-type warranties, the specific operational metric that is guaranteed (e.g., 'pump flow rate >= 500 GPM', 'motor efficiency >= 92%', 'membrane rejection rate >= 99%'). Null for parts/labor-only warranties.

The minimum or target numeric value of the performance guarantee metric that the asset must achieve under the warranty. For example, 500 (GPM) or 92 (%). Used to trigger performance warranty claims when asset output falls below the guaranteed level.
Unit of measure for the performance guarantee threshold value (e.g., GPM, MGD, PSI, %, NTU). Ensures correct interpretation of the numeric threshold in the context of the guaranteed metric.
The date the asset or component was purchased. Some warranties are triggered from purchase date rather than installation date; retained for accurate coverage period calculation.
The SAP purchase order number associated with the original procurement of the asset or component. Links the warranty to the procurement transaction for audit and claim substantiation.

The date the warranty was formally registered with the vendor or manufacturer. Some warranties require registration within a specified period post-purchase to be valid; failure to register may void coverage.

Indicates whether the warranty agreement includes an option to renew or extend coverage beyond the original expiration date. If True, renewal terms and pricing should be captured in the coverage_scope field.

Indicates whether the warranty terms require that all maintenance and repair work be performed exclusively by the original equipment manufacturer (OEM) or an OEM-authorized service provider. If True, routing non-OEM technicians to this asset may void the warranty.
The SAP PM maintenance order number associated with warranty-covered repair or maintenance work. Links warranty coverage to the SAP PM work order for cost recovery and OPEX tracking.
Indicates whether this warranty can be transferred to a new owner or operator in the event of asset sale, transfer, or organizational restructuring. Relevant for capital project asset handovers.
Email address for the designated warranty claims contact at the vendor or manufacturer. Used for formal claim submission and written correspondence.
Name of the designated warranty claims contact person at the vendor or manufacturer organization. Used to initiate and follow up on warranty claims.
Phone number for the designated warranty claims contact at the vendor or manufacturer. Used to initiate and follow up on warranty claims.

Externally-known warranty certificate or agreement number assigned by the vendor or manufacturer. Used for claim submission and correspondence with the warranty provider. Aligns with IBM Maximo warranty record identifier.

Current lifecycle state of the warranty record. 'active' indicates coverage is in force; 'expired' indicates the coverage period has ended; 'claimed' indicates a claim has been submitted and is under review; 'voided' indicates the warranty was invalidated (e.g., unauthorized repair); 'pending_activation' indicates the warranty has been registered but the coverage period has not yet started.

Classification of the warranty coverage scope. 'parts' covers replacement components only; 'labor' covers repair labor costs; 'performance' guarantees operational output levels (e.g., pump flow rate, motor efficiency); 'comprehensive' covers both parts and labor; 'extended' is a purchased extension beyond the original term; 'manufacturer' is the standard OEM warranty.

Foreign key linking to finance.cost_center. Business justification: Failure events trigger emergency repairs charged to cost centers for unplanned maintenance budget tracking. Critical for analyzing cost center budget variances and justifying contingency reserves in r
Reference to the physical asset that experienced the failure (equipment, pipe, pump, valve, etc.).
Reference to the physical asset that experienced the failure (equipment, pipe, pump, valve, etc.).

Foreign key linking to laboratory.lab_sample. Business justification: Failure investigations require water quality sampling to determine if contamination, corrosion, or water chemistry contributed to asset failure. Regulatory requirement for reportable incidents and sta

Reference to the employee who reported or documented the failure event.

Foreign key linking to quality.water_sample. Business justification: Asset failures (main breaks, tank overflows, pressure loss events) trigger mandatory water quality sampling per regulatory requirements (e.g., bacteriological sampling after pressure loss per TCR/RTCR
Reference to the corrective maintenance work order generated in response to this failure event.

business_glossary_term: Actual Repair Cost in US Dollars (USD)
description: Actual total cost incurred to repair or replace the failed asset after work completion.
The operational system or process affected by the failure (e.g., high service pump station, chlorination system, SCADA network, distribution main).
List of recommended corrective and preventive actions to prevent recurrence of similar failures.
Timestamp when this failure record was first created in the system.
Boolean flag indicating whether the failure resulted in a Combined Sewer Overflow event requiring regulatory reporting.
Count of customer accounts impacted by the failure event (service interruption, pressure loss, water quality issue).

Method by which the failure was discovered (SCADA alarm, operator inspection, customer complaint, etc.). [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: scada_alarm|operator_inspection|customer_complaint|scheduled_inspection|predictive_maintenance|routine_testing|emergency_response — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Total duration in hours that the asset was out of service due to the failure.
Detailed description of immediate emergency response actions taken to mitigate the failure (e.g., valve isolation, backup system activation, customer notification).
Description of any environmental impact resulting from the failure (e.g., water body contamination, soil contamination, habitat damage).
Identified root cause of the failure (e.g., age-related deterioration, improper installation, inadequate maintenance, design deficiency, operational error, external damage).
Calculated criticality score based on failure severity, service impact, and asset importance (typically 1-100 scale).
Calendar date when the asset failure occurred.
Description of the operational impact and consequences of the failure on system performance and service delivery.
Classification of how the asset failed (e.g., mechanical fracture, electrical short, corrosion perforation, seal leakage, bearing seizure, control malfunction).
Business-readable unique identifier for the failure event, typically auto-generated by CMMS.
Severity classification of the failure based on service impact, safety risk, and regulatory implications.
Current lifecycle status of the failure record in the investigation and resolution workflow.
Precise date and time when the asset failure was detected or occurred.
Timestamp when this failure record was last updated or modified.

business_glossary_term: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) Impact Indicator
description: Boolean flag indicating whether this failure event should be included in MTBF reliability calculations for the asset class.

business_glossary_term: Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) in Hours
description: Measured time in hours from failure detection to restoration of asset to full operational status.
Additional free-text notes, observations, or context related to the failure event.
Estimated volume of wastewater overflow in gallons resulting from SSO or CSO event, if applicable.

business_glossary_term: System Pressure Drop in Pounds per Square Inch (PSI)
description: Measured drop in system pressure (PSI) resulting from the failure event.

business_glossary_term: Production Loss in Million Gallons per Day (MGD)
description: Estimated water production capacity lost due to the failure, measured in Million Gallons per Day.
Date when regulatory agencies were notified of the failure event, if applicable.
Boolean flag indicating whether the failure event requires notification to regulatory agencies (EPA, state primacy agency).

business_glossary_term: Estimated Repair Cost in US Dollars (USD)
description: Estimated total cost to repair or replace the failed asset, including labor, materials, and contractor costs.
Date when the failure was fully resolved and the asset was restored to normal operation.
Boolean flag indicating whether a formal root cause analysis (RCA) has been completed for this failure event.
Detailed findings and conclusions from the root cause analysis investigation, including contributing factors and systemic issues identified.
Boolean flag indicating whether the failure resulted in a complete service interruption to customers.
Boolean flag indicating whether the failure resulted in a Sanitary Sewer Overflow event requiring regulatory reporting.
Boolean flag indicating whether the failure is covered under manufacturer or contractor warranty and a claim was filed.

Method used by the CMMS to compute the average_reading_rate. SLIDING_WINDOW: rolling average over a configurable number of recent readings. FIXED_PERIOD: average over a fixed calendar period. LAST_READING: uses only the most recent reading interval.

Current calibration status of the meter instrument. CALIBRATED: within valid calibration period. DUE: calibration due within the next calibration_interval_days. OVERDUE: calibration date has passed without recalibration. IN_PROGRESS: currently being calibrated. NOT_REQUIRED: meter type does not require formal calibration (e.g., simple cycle counters).

Acceptable measurement tolerance or accuracy specification for this meter expressed in the meter's unit of measure. Readings outside this tolerance trigger recalibration. For example, ±0.5 PSI for a pressure transducer or ±0.01 MGD for a flow totalizer.
Timestamp when the asset meter record was first created in the enterprise data platform. Supports audit trail and data lineage requirements. Formatted as yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.

The most recent recorded value of the meter or counter as of the last_reading_date. For CONTINUOUS meters this is the cumulative total; for GAUGE meters this is the last observed point-in-time value. Principal quantitative fact for this MASTER_RESOURCE.
Date on which the meter was permanently removed from service and retired. Null for active meters. Used for lifecycle reporting and asset history.
Date on which the meter was physically installed on the asset. Used to calculate meter age, warranty expiry, and to establish the baseline reading at installation.

Indicates whether readings from this meter are used in regulatory compliance reporting (e.g., EPA NPDES Discharge Monitoring Reports, Safe Drinking Water Act compliance, Monthly Operating Reports). True = regulatory-grade meter subject to calibration and data integrity requirements.

Date on which the meter was most recently calibrated and certified as accurate. Critical for regulatory compliance where metered values (e.g., flow, pressure, chemical dosing) are used in EPA/NPDES reporting.

business_glossary_term: Last Meter Reading Date and Time
description: Timestamp of the most recent meter reading captured, whether from SCADA/OSIsoft PI Historian automated feed or manual entry in IBM Maximo. Formatted as yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.

Free-text description of the physical location where the meter is installed on the asset, e.g., 'Discharge side of Pump 3 at WTP Building A', 'Inlet valve actuator shaft'. Supplements the parent asset location for field technician navigation.
Name of the manufacturer of the meter instrument or sensor hardware, e.g., Endress+Hauser, Yokogawa, Emerson, Siemens. Used for procurement, warranty tracking, and spare parts management.

Engineering unit of measure for the meter reading, e.g., HOURS (run-hours), STARTS (motor starts), CYCLES (valve cycles), MGD (Million Gallons per Day), GPM (Gallons per Minute), PSI (Pounds per Square Inch), NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units), RPM, DEGREES_F, INCHES. Aligns with OSIsoft PI Historian tag engineering units.
Human-readable descriptive name for the meter or counter, e.g., 'Pump 3 Run Hours', 'Valve 12 Cycle Count', 'Influent Flow Totalizer'. Used for display in CMMS work orders and PM triggers.

Externally-known unique alphanumeric identifier for the meter as assigned in IBM Maximo CMMS or SAP PM. Used for cross-system reference and field identification. Distinct from customer billing meter numbers managed by the Metering domain.

Current operational lifecycle status of the meter. ACTIVE: in service and collecting readings. INACTIVE: temporarily not in use. RETIRED: permanently decommissioned. SUSPENDED: readings paused (e.g., asset out of service). PENDING_CALIBRATION: due for or undergoing calibration.

Classification of the meter per IBM Maximo meter type taxonomy. CONTINUOUS: accumulating counter that never resets (e.g., run-hours, cycle counts, flow totalizer). GAUGE: point-in-time measurement that can go up or down (e.g., PSI, vibration amplitude, temperature). CHARACTERISTIC: qualitative or categorical observation (e.g., condition rating, oil color).

business_glossary_term: Reference to single source of truth in metering.metering_meter
description: Foreign key to the canonical metering_meter record in metering domain
Manufacturer's model or part number for the meter instrument. Used for procurement, spare parts identification, and technical documentation lookup.
Scheduled date by which the meter must next be calibrated to maintain measurement accuracy and regulatory compliance. Drives calibration work order generation in IBM Maximo.

The OSIsoft PI Historian tag identifier associated with this meter for automated SCADA data ingestion. Enables direct linkage between the CMMS meter record and the PI Historian time-series data stream. Null if the meter is not integrated with PI Historian.

The recurring interval of meter units between successive PM work order generations. For example, every 500 run-hours or every 1000 valve cycles. Works in conjunction with pm_trigger_threshold for ongoing PM scheduling.

The meter reading value at which a Preventive Maintenance (PM) work order is automatically generated. For example, a pump PM triggered every 2000 run-hours. Core field enabling meter-based PM scheduling in IBM Maximo.
The meter reading value recorded immediately prior to the current reading. Used to calculate consumption delta between readings and to detect anomalous jumps or rollover events.

business_glossary_term: Previous Meter Reading Date and Time
description: Timestamp of the meter reading that preceded the current reading. Used in conjunction with previous_reading to compute usage rate and validate reading intervals.

Expected frequency at which meter readings are collected. CONTINUOUS: real-time SCADA/PI Historian streaming. HOURLY/DAILY/WEEKLY/MONTHLY: scheduled batch collection. ON_DEMAND: readings taken only when required (e.g., during inspections or work orders).

Maximum valid reading value for this meter as defined by the instrument's operating range or process design limits. Readings above this value are flagged as suspect or trigger alarms. For example, 150 PSI maximum for a distribution pressure transducer.

Minimum valid reading value for this meter as defined by the instrument's operating range or process design limits. Readings below this value are flagged as suspect. For example, 0 PSI minimum for a pressure transducer.

The system or method by which the current meter reading was captured. SCADA: from Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition system. PI_HISTORIAN: from OSIsoft PI Historian automated feed. MANUAL: entered manually by a technician in IBM Maximo. AMI: from Advanced Metering Infrastructure. CMMS_IMPORT: batch import from CMMS. FIELD_DEVICE: direct PLC/RTU feed.

The regulatory parameter code or identifier associated with this meter for compliance reporting submissions, e.g., EPA DMR parameter code, NPDES permit parameter identifier, or state drinking water program monitoring code. Null if is_regulatory_meter is False.

Free-text field for operational notes, technician observations, or special instructions related to this meter. For example, notes about known reading anomalies, temporary bypass conditions, or pending replacement. Sourced from IBM Maximo meter remarks field.

The maximum value the meter counter can reach before rolling over to zero. Applicable to CONTINUOUS meters with fixed-digit displays (e.g., 99999.9 hours). Used to correctly compute cumulative usage across rollover events. Null if the meter has no rollover limit.

business_glossary_term: SAP Plant Maintenance (PM) Measuring Point ID
description: The measuring point identifier from SAP Plant Maintenance (PM) module corresponding to this asset meter. Enables reconciliation between IBM Maximo CMMS and SAP PM for assets managed across both systems.
Manufacturer's serial number uniquely identifying the physical meter instrument. Used for warranty claims, calibration certificates, and asset tracking.

Timestamp when the asset meter record was most recently modified in the enterprise data platform. Used for change tracking, incremental data loads, and audit compliance. Formatted as yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.

Name or identifier of the person, team, or system that performed the criticality assessment. Supports audit trail and accountability for rating decisions.
Relative priority ranking for capital renewal or replacement within the asset portfolio. Lower numbers indicate higher priority. Derived from criticality tier and overall risk score.

Quantitative score representing the business, operational, safety, environmental, and regulatory impact if the asset fails. Typically scaled 1-100 or 1-10 depending on methodology.

Categorical classification of asset criticality derived from the overall risk score. Determines maintenance frequency, inspection intervals, and capital renewal priority.

Indicates whether failure of this asset would result in environmental harm such as discharge of untreated wastewater, contamination of water sources, or SSO/CSO events. True if environmental impact is likely.
Estimated financial consequence of asset failure including emergency repair costs, lost revenue, regulatory fines, and liability claims. Expressed in USD.

Timestamp when this criticality rating record was last updated. Supports change tracking and audit trail requirements.
Quantitative score representing the probability of asset failure based on age, condition, maintenance history, and operating environment. Typically scaled 1-100 or 1-10 depending on methodology.

Version identifier of the criticality assessment methodology used to calculate this rating. Enables tracking of methodology changes over time and ensures consistent application across asset portfolio.
Scheduled date for the next criticality reassessment. Critical assets typically require more frequent review cycles than low-criticality assets.

Composite risk score calculated from Consequence of Failure and Likelihood of Failure scores. Typically computed as CoF × LoF. Used to rank assets for maintenance and capital investment prioritization.
Recommended interval in days between preventive maintenance activities based on the criticality tier. Critical assets require more frequent PM than low-criticality assets.

Indicates whether failure of this asset would pose a direct threat to public health through loss of water supply, water quality degradation, or exposure to contaminants. True if public health risk exists.
The date from which this criticality rating becomes effective and is used for maintenance prioritization and capital planning decisions.
The date when this criticality rating expires and must be reassessed. Nullable for ratings without a defined review cycle.
Current lifecycle status of the criticality rating record. Active ratings are used for operational decisions; superseded ratings are retained for historical analysis.
Indicates whether backup or redundant assets are available to maintain service if this asset fails. True if redundancy exists, False if asset is a single point of failure.
Indicates whether failure of this asset would result in regulatory non-compliance, permit violations, or mandatory reporting under SDWA, CWA, or NPDES. True if regulatory consequences exist.
Standard interval in months between criticality reassessments for this asset. Typically shorter for critical assets and longer for low-criticality assets.

Indicates whether failure of this asset would create safety hazards for employees, contractors, or the public (e.g., chemical release, electrical hazard, structural collapse). True if safety risk exists.
Estimated duration in hours that service would be disrupted if this asset fails, accounting for repair time, parts availability, and system restoration procedures.

Reference to the CIP project that triggered the asset replacement and disposal, if applicable. Links disposal to capital planning and renewal programs.
Reference to the asset being disposed from the asset registry. Links to the physical infrastructure asset (pipe, pump, valve, equipment, vehicle) that is being retired or decommissioned.
Reference to the new asset that replaced the disposed asset, if disposal reason was replacement. Links retired asset to its successor for lifecycle tracking.

The total depreciation expense accumulated on the asset from installation date through disposal date. Used in calculating book value and gain/loss on disposal.
The date on which the disposal transaction was formally approved by the authorizing approver. Required before disposal can proceed.

Name or identifier of the authorizing approver who approved the asset disposal. Typically a senior manager, director, or capital planning committee member with authority over asset retirement decisions.
The net book value (original acquisition cost minus accumulated depreciation) of the asset at the time of disposal. Used to calculate gain or loss on disposal for financial reporting.
Contact information (phone, email, or address) for the buyer of the asset. Used for transfer documentation and warranty transfer. Confidential business contact data.
Name of the individual or organization that purchased the asset, if disposal method was sale or auction. Null for other disposal methods.

The date on which the asset was officially retired or disposed. This is the principal business event date that triggers SAP FI-AA asset retirement posting and updates the asset registry operational status to Retired.

business_glossary_term: Static Pressure (PSI - Pounds per Square Inch)

business_glossary_term: Minimum Night Flow (MNF) Threshold in Gallons per Minute (GPM)

Scheduled date for the next active leakage detection survey within the DMA. Used for planning and resource allocation.
Free-text field for operational notes, special instructions, historical context, or any additional information relevant to the DMA management and monitoring.

Target threshold for Non-Revenue Water as a percentage of total water supplied to the DMA. NRW includes physical losses (leakage) and commercial losses (metering inaccuracies, theft). Typical industry targets range from 10% to 20%.

Target threshold for Unaccounted-for Water as a percentage of total water supplied. UFW is a broader measure than NRW and includes all water that cannot be accounted for through billing or authorized use.

Reference to the District Metered Area (DMA) containing this valve. DMAs are isolated network sections with defined boundaries and metered inflows/outflows, used for Non-Revenue Water (NRW) and Unaccounted-for Water (UFW) analysis. Valves on DMA boundaries are critical for isolation and flow control.

Reference to the asset registry record in the Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS). Links this valve to IBM Maximo Asset Management for maintenance tracking, work orders, and lifecycle management.

Reference to the asset registry record in the Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS). Links this valve to IBM Maximo Asset Management for maintenance tracking, work orders, and lifecycle management.
Reference to the water main on which this valve is installed. Links the valve to the pipe segment for network topology modeling in Esri ArcGIS and Innovyze InfoWater.

Reference to the pressure zone in which this valve is located. Pressure zones are geographic areas maintained at specific pressure ranges (measured in Pounds per Square Inch - PSI) to ensure adequate service and prevent pipe bursts. Critical for hydraulic modeling in Innovyze InfoWater.

Foreign key linking to supply.vendor. Business justification: Valve suppliers tracked for approved vendor list management, quality assurance, delivery performance, and warranty management. Essential for procurement compliance and equipment standardization progra

Depth in feet from ground surface to the valve operating nut. Used for excavation planning, valve box sizing, and accessibility assessment. Typical range is 3-8 feet depending on frost line and main depth.
City or municipality in which the valve is located. Used for jurisdictional reporting and geographic analysis.

Assessment of the valve's physical condition based on the most recent inspection. Excellent: like new, no defects; Good: minor wear, fully functional; Fair: moderate wear, functional with minor issues; Poor: significant deterioration, may fail soon; Critical: immediate replacement required. Used for risk-based asset management and Capital Expenditure (CAPEX) planning.
Timestamp when this valve record was first created in the system. Used for data lineage, audit trails, and compliance with data governance policies.

Business criticality of the valve based on its role in the distribution network. Critical valves control large trunk mains or serve hospitals/fire protection; high criticality valves isolate major zones; medium and low criticality valves serve smaller areas. Drives maintenance prioritization and replacement sequencing.
Actual current position of the valve as of the last field verification or SCADA reading. May differ from normal_position during maintenance, emergencies, or operational adjustments.
Nominal diameter of the valve in inches. Critical for hydraulic modeling in Innovyze InfoWater and flow capacity calculations. Common sizes range from 2 to 48 inches in distribution networks.

Planned frequency in months for valve exercising activities. Typically 12 months for standard valves, 6 months for critical isolation valves, and 24 months for low-priority valves. Drives preventive maintenance scheduling in CMMS.
Unique feature identifier in the Esri ArcGIS system. Links this valve record to the corresponding GIS feature layer for spatial analysis, map display, and network topology modeling.

Date the valve was originally installed in the distribution network. Used for age-based asset management, depreciation calculations, and replacement planning under the Capital Improvement Program (CIP).
Year the valve was installed. Derived from installation_date for simplified age analysis and reporting when exact date is not required.

Indicates whether the valve is buried underground (True) or above ground in a vault or building (False). Buried valves require valve box access and are more difficult to exercise; above-ground valves are more accessible but require weather protection.
Indicates whether the valve is equipped with a motor or actuator for remote operation. True for SCADA-controlled valves; False for manual valves requiring field crew operation.
Name or identifier of the crew member or contractor who last exercised the valve. Supports accountability and quality assurance in valve maintenance programs.

Date the valve was last exercised (opened and closed through its full range of motion). Regular valve exercising prevents seizing and ensures operability during emergencies. AWWA recommends annual exercising for critical valves.
Geographic latitude coordinate of the valve location in decimal degrees. Used for Geographic Information System (GIS) mapping in Esri ArcGIS, field crew navigation, and spatial analysis.
Geographic longitude coordinate of the valve location in decimal degrees. Used for Geographic Information System (GIS) mapping in Esri ArcGIS, field crew navigation, and spatial analysis.

Primary construction material of the valve body. Ductile iron is most common for large distribution valves; bronze and brass for smaller service valves; stainless steel for corrosive environments; PVC for low-pressure applications.
Timestamp when this valve record was last modified. Supports change tracking, audit requirements, and data quality monitoring.

Standard operating position of the valve under normal conditions. Critical for hydraulic modeling, isolation planning, and Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) monitoring. Most distribution valves are normally open; some isolation and control valves are normally closed or throttled.

Free-text field for additional operational notes, special instructions, access restrictions, or historical information about the valve. Examples: 'Requires two-person crew due to tight turns', 'Located in private easement, coordinate access', 'Replaced stem in 2018'.

business_glossary_term: Valve Operating Pressure (Pounds per Square Inch - PSI)
description: Normal operating pressure at the valve location in Pounds per Square Inch (PSI). Used for hydraulic modeling, pressure zone verification, and valve sizing validation. Typical distribution system pressures range from 40-120 PSI.

Current lifecycle status of the valve in the distribution network. Active valves are in service; inactive valves are temporarily out of service; abandoned valves are no longer used but not removed; removed valves have been physically extracted; planned valves are scheduled for installation.
Postal code of the valve location. Supports geographic segmentation and service area analysis.

business_glossary_term: Valve Pressure Rating (Pounds per Square Inch - PSI)
description: Maximum rated working pressure of the valve in Pounds per Square Inch (PSI) as specified by the manufacturer. Must exceed operating_pressure_psi with adequate safety margin. Common ratings are 150, 200, 250, and 300 PSI.

business_glossary_term: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) Tag
description: SCADA system tag identifier for automated valves with remote monitoring and control capability. Links to OSIsoft PI Historian for real-time position monitoring, alarm management, and operational analytics. Only populated for motorized or actuated valves integrated with SCADA.
State or province in which the valve is located. Used for regulatory reporting to State Drinking Water Programs and Primacy Agencies.
Nearest street address or intersection to the valve location. Provides human-readable location reference for field crews, emergency responders, and customer service representatives.

Number of complete turns required to fully close the valve from the fully open position. Used by field crews during valve exercising programs and emergency isolation procedures. Typical range is 5-50 turns depending on valve size and type.

Expected useful life of the valve in years from installation date. Used for depreciation calculations per Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) and Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB) standards, and for long-term replacement planning. Typical range is 50-75 years for distribution valves.

Type of valve box or access structure protecting the buried valve. Standard boxes for sidewalk/lawn areas; traffic-rated boxes for roadways; extension boxes for deep valves; vaults for large valves; none for above-ground installations.

Primary operational function of the valve in the distribution network. Isolation valves segment the network for maintenance; control valves regulate flow; pressure reducing valves (PRV) manage pressure zones; check valves prevent backflow; air release valves expel trapped air; blowoff valves drain sections.
Externally-known business identifier for the valve, typically painted or tagged on the valve in the field. Used by operations and maintenance crews for identification.

Classification of the valve mechanism. Gate valves provide full flow with minimal pressure drop; butterfly valves are compact and quick-operating; ball valves offer tight shutoff; check valves prevent backflow; plug, cone, and needle valves provide throttling control. Reference to the pressure zone served by this storage tank. Pressure zones are geographic areas of the distribution network maintained at similar hydraulic pressure ranges.

Foreign key linking to asset.asset_registry. Business justification: Storage tanks are major capital assets with regulatory inspection requirements (AWWA D100), condition assessments, coating schedules, and depreciation tracking. Essential for asset management and fina

Risk-based criticality classification of the storage tank based on consequence of failure analysis considering population served, redundancy, and system impact: critical (single point of failure for large population), high (significant impact), medium (moderate impact), or low (minimal impact with redundancy available).
Ground or foundation elevation (in feet above mean sea level or local datum) at the base of the storage tank structure. Used for hydraulic gradient calculations.
Total storage capacity of the tank measured in gallons. Represents the maximum volume of potable water the tank can hold at overflow elevation.
Total storage capacity expressed in million gallons (MG), the standard unit for water utility storage reporting and system adequacy analysis.

Assessment of the current condition of the tank's protective coating system based on the most recent inspection: excellent (no defects), good (minor wear), fair (localized deterioration), poor (widespread deterioration), or failed (coating breakdown requiring immediate attention).
Volume of water (in gallons) reserved for emergency supply during system outages, treatment plant failures, or other contingencies. Separate from fire flow reserve and operational storage.
Volume of water (in gallons) reserved in the storage tank to meet fire protection requirements and emergency fire flow demands as defined by local fire codes and insurance standards.
Unique feature identifier from the Esri ArcGIS system linking this storage tank record to its spatial representation in the GIS network model.
Node identifier in the Innovyze InfoWater hydraulic model representing this storage tank. Used for network simulation, pressure analysis, and system optimization studies.
Diameter (in inches) of the primary inlet pipe supplying water to the storage tank. Used for hydraulic modeling and flow capacity analysis.
Date when the storage tank was originally constructed and placed into service. Used for asset age calculation and depreciation schedules.
Date when the storage tank interior was last drained, cleaned, and disinfected. Regular cleaning is required to maintain water quality and prevent sediment accumulation.
Date when the interior or exterior protective coating was last applied or rehabilitated. Coating maintenance is critical for corrosion prevention and structural longevity.
Date of the most recent comprehensive inspection of the storage tank, including structural integrity, coating condition, and safety systems. Required for regulatory compliance and asset management.
Geographic latitude coordinate (decimal degrees) of the storage tank location. Used for GIS mapping, spatial analysis, and integration with Esri ArcGIS.
Geographic longitude coordinate (decimal degrees) of the storage tank location. Used for GIS mapping, spatial analysis, and integration with Esri ArcGIS.
Asset identifier from IBM Maximo Asset Management (CMMS) system linking this storage tank to its maintenance history, work orders, preventive maintenance schedules, and spare parts inventory.
Highest water level (in feet) at which the tank should operate under normal conditions, typically set below overflow elevation to provide freeboard and prevent overflow events.
Lowest water level (in feet) at which the tank should operate under normal conditions to maintain adequate system pressure and prevent pump cavitation or structural stress.
Indicates whether an active mixing system is installed in the storage tank to prevent water age stratification and maintain disinfectant residual throughout the tank volume.
Type of mixing system installed: mechanical (motor-driven mixer), hydraulic (jet mixing using inlet flow), or none (no active mixing).
Scheduled date for the next required comprehensive inspection based on regulatory requirements, manufacturer recommendations, or utility inspection frequency policy.
Free-form text field for additional operational notes, special conditions, historical information, or other relevant details about the storage tank not captured in structured fields.

Current operational state of the storage tank in the distribution network: in-service (actively storing and supplying water), out-of-service (temporarily offline), standby (available but not actively used), under-maintenance (undergoing inspection or repair), or decommissioned (permanently retired).
Diameter (in inches) of the primary outlet pipe distributing water from the storage tank to the distribution network. Used for hydraulic modeling and flow capacity analysis.
Elevation (in feet above mean sea level or local datum) at which the tank overflow pipe is located. Represents the absolute maximum water level before overflow discharge occurs.
Diameter (in inches) of the overflow pipe that prevents tank overfilling by discharging excess water when the maximum level is reached.

Legal ownership classification of the storage tank: utility-owned (owned and operated by the water utility), leased (leased from another entity), shared (jointly owned with another utility or municipality), or third-party (owned by external entity with service agreement).
Timestamp when this storage tank record was first created in the system. Used for data lineage and audit trail purposes.
Timestamp when this storage tank record was last modified. Used for data lineage, change tracking, and audit trail purposes.

Current compliance status with state drinking water program and EPA inspection requirements: compliant (meets all requirements), non-compliant (deficiencies identified), pending-review (inspection submitted awaiting approval), or not-applicable (exempt from inspection requirements).

business_glossary_term: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) Flow Meter Tag
description: OSIsoft PI Historian tag name for the flow meter measuring inflow or outflow (GPM or MGD) from this storage tank. Used for demand analysis and water balance calculations.

business_glossary_term: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) Level Sensor Tag
description: OSIsoft PI Historian tag name for the real-time water level sensor monitoring this storage tank. Used to retrieve current level, historical trends, and alarm conditions from the SCADA system.

business_glossary_term: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) Pressure Sensor Tag
description: OSIsoft PI Historian tag name for the pressure sensor monitoring outlet pressure (PSI) from this storage tank. Used for hydraulic performance monitoring and pressure zone management.

Indicates whether physical security systems (fencing, locks, intrusion detection, surveillance cameras) are installed to protect the storage tank from unauthorized access and potential contamination threats.

Overall structural integrity assessment of the storage tank based on the most recent inspection: excellent (no defects), good (minor issues), fair (moderate deterioration), poor (significant deterioration requiring repair), or critical (unsafe condition requiring immediate action).
Primary construction material of the storage tank structure: steel (welded or bolted), concrete (cast-in-place or precast), prestressed concrete, composite (steel and concrete), or fiberglass.
Common name or designation of the storage tank, often referencing geographic location or service area (e.g., 'Hillside Elevated Tank', 'Downtown Reservoir').
Business identifier or asset tag assigned to the storage tank for operational reference and field identification.

Classification of storage tank by structural configuration: elevated (water tower), ground-level (surface reservoir), standpipe (tall cylindrical), reservoir (large capacity ground storage), clearwell (treated water storage at WTP), or hydropneumatic (pressure tank).
Effective storage capacity available for distribution operations, calculated as the volume between minimum operating level and overflow elevation. Excludes dead storage below minimum operating level.

The first day of the reporting period for this water balance calculation. Typically the first day of a month or year.

business_glossary_term: Current Annual Real Losses (CARL) (Million Gallons - MG)
description: Annualized volume of real losses (physical leakage) in the distribution system, measured in million gallons per year. Used in the calculation of Infrastructure Leakage Index.
Volume of water consumed but not registered by customer meters due to meter under-registration, measured in million gallons. Typically estimated based on meter age and testing data.

business_glossary_term: Leakage on Service Connections (Million Gallons - MG)
description: Volume of water lost through leaks on service connections from the main to the customer meter, measured in million gallons. Includes leaks on both utility-owned and customer-owned portions.

business_glossary_term: Leakage and Overflow on Storage Tanks (Million Gallons - MG)
description: Volume of water lost through leaks and overflows at storage tanks and reservoirs, measured in million gallons. Includes structural leaks and operational overflows.

business_glossary_term: Leakage on Transmission and Distribution Mains (Million Gallons - MG)
description: Volume of water lost through leaks and breaks on transmission and distribution mains, measured in million gallons. Includes both reported and unreported leaks.

Balance notes
Non-Revenue Water expressed as a percentage of system input volume. Calculated as (NRW Volume / System Input Volume) × 100. Industry benchmark for water loss performance.

Total volume of water that does not generate revenue for the utility, calculated as system input volume minus billed authorized consumption, measured in million gallons. Key performance indicator for water loss management.
Number of service connections

Volume of water physically lost from the distribution system through leaks, breaks, and overflows on mains, service connections, and storage tanks, measured in million gallons.
Total number of active service connections in the system or DMA during the audit period. Used in UARL calculation and for normalizing leakage metrics.

Total volume of water introduced into the distribution system during the audit period, measured in million gallons. Represents the sum of all water entering the system from treatment plants, wells, and purchased sources.
Total length of transmission and distribution mains in the system or DMA, measured in miles. Used in UARL calculation and for normalizing leakage metrics.

Legacy term for water losses, calculated as system input volume minus authorized consumption, measured in million gallons. Equivalent to water losses in modern AWWA methodology.
Volume of water consumed through illegal connections, meter tampering, or theft, measured in million gallons. Estimated based on field investigations and industry benchmarks.

business_glossary_term: Unavoidable Annual Real Losses (UARL) (Million Gallons - MG)
description: Theoretical minimum achievable annual real losses for a well-maintained and well-managed system, calculated based on system characteristics (main length, service connections, pressure). Used as the denominator in ILI calculation.
Volume of water used for authorized purposes but not billed, such as firefighting, main flushing, street cleaning, and utility operations, measured in million gallons.
Total volume of water lost in the distribution system, calculated as system input volume minus authorized consumption, measured in million gallons. Comprises apparent losses and real losses.
Unique identifier for the flow measurement record. Primary key for the flow reading transaction.

Reference to the physical location or asset where the flow measurement was captured (DMA inlet/outlet meter, pump station discharge meter, PRV station meter, or bulk transfer point).
Reference to the specific flow meter device that captured this reading. Links to asset registry for meter calibration history and maintenance records.
Indicates whether this flow reading triggered an alarm condition in the SCADA system (e.g., flow exceeds threshold, negative flow, meter communication failure).

Indicates whether this flow reading is used for bulk water billing or wholesale customer invoicing (e.g., inter-utility transfers, industrial bulk customers).
Date of the most recent meter calibration prior to this reading. Used to assess measurement reliability and schedule recalibration.
Free-text field for operator notes, validation comments, or explanations of anomalies in the flow reading. Used for audit trail and troubleshooting.

Quality indicator for the flow reading. Good = validated measurement, Suspect = questionable but not rejected, Bad = failed validation, Estimated = calculated/interpolated value, Manual = operator-entered reading.

Unit of measure for the flow reading. GPM = Gallons per Minute, MGD = Million Gallons per Day, CFS = Cubic Feet per Second, LPS = Liters per Second, M3H = Cubic Meters per Hour, M3D = Cubic Meters per Day.
Indicates whether the flow value is an estimated or interpolated value rather than a direct meter reading. True when meter communication fails or reading is missing.
Method used to estimate the flow value when direct measurement is unavailable. None indicates a direct measured value.
Direction of water flow at the measurement point. Inflow = water entering the zone/DMA, Outflow = water leaving the zone/DMA, Bidirectional = flow can reverse direction.

The raw flow measurement value as captured by the meter. Represents instantaneous flow rate or cumulative volume depending on measurement type. Used with engineering_unit to interpret the measurement.
Indicates whether this flow reading is used for hydraulic model calibration in Innovyze InfoWater or similar distribution network modeling software.
Time interval in minutes over which the flow measurement was aggregated or averaged. Common values: 15, 30, 60 minutes for SCADA polling intervals.

Classification of the flow measurement: instantaneous (real-time snapshot), cumulative (totalizer reading), average (calculated over interval), peak (maximum in interval), or minimum (lowest in interval).
The rated accuracy of the flow meter at the time of this reading, expressed as a percentage. Used to calculate measurement uncertainty for water balance calculations.
Indicates whether this flow reading is included in Non-Revenue Water (NRW) or Unaccounted-for Water (UFW) balance calculations for the associated DMA.

Date and time when this flow reading record was first inserted into the data system. Used for data lineage and audit trail.
Date and time when this flow reading record was last modified. Used for change tracking and audit trail.

business_glossary_term: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) Tag Name
description: The SCADA system tag or point identifier that sourced this flow reading. Used for traceability back to the PI Historian or SCADA historian database.
Water temperature in degrees Fahrenheit at the measurement point. Used for flow compensation calculations and water quality correlation analysis.
Cumulative volume reading from the meter totalizer register. Used to calculate interval consumption by differencing consecutive readings. Typically in gallons or cubic meters.
User ID or system process name that performed the validation of this flow reading. Used for audit trail and accountability.

Current validation state of the flow reading. Pending = awaiting review, Validated = approved by operator or automated validation, Rejected = failed validation rules, Corrected = manually adjusted after validation.
Date and time when the flow reading was validated or reviewed by an operator or automated validation process.

Name of the engineer or analyst who executed and is responsible for this hydraulic model run, used for accountability and technical review.

business_glossary_term: Average Pressure (PSI - Pounds per Square Inch)
description: Mean nodal pressure in pounds per square inch (PSI) across all nodes in the distribution network during the simulation, providing an overall system pressure indicator.
Description of hydraulic boundary conditions applied to the model, including source pressures, tank levels, pump operating rules, and valve settings that define the system state for this simulation.

Indicates the level of calibration and validation applied to the hydraulic model for this run: not calibrated (theoretical), preliminary (initial tuning), calibrated (field-verified), or validated (peer-reviewed and accepted).
Boolean indicator of whether the hydraulic solver achieved numerical convergence within tolerance limits, ensuring the simulation results are mathematically valid and stable.
Number of solver iterations required to achieve hydraulic convergence, used to assess model complexity and computational efficiency.
Date and time when this hydraulic model run record was first created in the system, supporting audit trail and data lineage tracking.

Scaling factor applied to baseline water demand values to simulate different loading conditions (e.g., 1.0 for average day, 1.5 for peak day, 2.5 for maximum day plus fire flow). Used for capacity planning and stress testing.

business_glossary_term: Fire Flow Available (GPM - Gallons per Minute)
description: Maximum fire flow capacity in gallons per minute (GPM) available at critical nodes while maintaining minimum residual pressure, used for fire protection adequacy assessment and Insurance Services Office (ISO) rating.

business_glossary_term: Geographic Information System (GIS) Model Synchronization Date
description: Date when the hydraulic model network topology was last synchronized with the authoritative Esri ArcGIS geographic information system (GIS) database, ensuring spatial accuracy and asset alignment.
Date and time when this hydraulic model run record was last updated, supporting change tracking and data governance.

business_glossary_term: Maximum Pressure (PSI - Pounds per Square Inch)
description: Highest nodal pressure in pounds per square inch (PSI) observed across the entire distribution network during the simulation, used to identify areas at risk of pipe bursts or excessive pressure.
Highest water velocity in feet per second (fps) observed in any pipe segment during the simulation, used to identify areas at risk of excessive head loss, water hammer, or pipe erosion.

business_glossary_term: Minimum Pressure (PSI - Pounds per Square Inch)
description: Lowest nodal pressure in pounds per square inch (PSI) observed across the entire distribution network during the simulation, critical for identifying areas at risk of low pressure or negative pressure events.
Lowest water velocity in feet per second (fps) observed in any pipe segment during the simulation, used to identify areas with stagnant water or inadequate flushing velocity.
Version identifier of the hydraulic model used for this simulation run, tracking model evolution and configuration changes over time.
Free-text field for analyst comments, observations, assumptions, or special conditions related to this hydraulic model run, supporting documentation and knowledge transfer.
File system path or cloud storage location where detailed simulation output files (node results, pipe results, time-series data) are stored for this hydraulic model run.

Number of nodes where pressure fell below the minimum regulatory threshold (typically 20 PSI under normal conditions or 30 PSI during fire flow) during the simulation, indicating potential compliance issues.
Total electrical energy consumed by all pump stations in kilowatt-hours (kWh) during the simulation period, used for operational cost analysis and energy efficiency optimization.
Date and time when the hydraulic model simulation run completed execution, whether successfully or with errors.
Business-facing identifier or reference number for the hydraulic model run, used for tracking and reporting purposes.

Business purpose or objective for executing this hydraulic model run, such as Capital Improvement Program (CIP) planning, regulatory compliance analysis, operational troubleshooting, or infrastructure design validation.
Date and time when the hydraulic model simulation run was initiated by the analyst or automated scheduler.

Current execution status of the hydraulic model run, indicating whether the simulation is pending, in progress, successfully completed with convergence, failed due to errors, or cancelled by the user. description: Identifier of the SCADA system or OSIsoft PI Historian data source used to calibrate or validate this hydraulic model run with real-time operational data.

Type of hydraulic simulation scenario executed: steady-state for snapshot conditions, extended period simulation (EPS) for time-series analysis, fire flow for emergency capacity, water quality for contaminant tracking, emergency response for outage planning, or capacity planning for infrastructure investment analysis.
Total elapsed time in seconds for the hydraulic model simulation to execute, used for performance monitoring and optimization.
Simulated real-world end date and time for extended period simulation (EPS) scenarios, representing the conclusion of the modeled time window.
Simulated real-world start date and time for extended period simulation (EPS) scenarios, representing the beginning of the modeled time window.

business_glossary_term: System Demand (MGD - Million Gallons per Day)
description: Total water demand across the entire distribution system in million gallons per day (MGD) for the simulation scenario, representing aggregate customer consumption and system losses.
Maximum range of water level fluctuation in feet across all storage tanks during the simulation, indicating storage utilization and operational cycling.
Temporal resolution in minutes for extended period simulation, defining the interval at which hydraulic conditions are calculated (e.g., 15, 30, or 60 minutes).
Cumulative hydraulic head loss in feet across the distribution network from source to the most remote node, indicating overall system friction and energy dissipation.
Number of pipe segments where velocity exceeded recommended maximum thresholds (typically 5-8 fps) during the simulation, indicating potential water quality or infrastructure integrity concerns.

Date on which the laboratory supervisor or quality manager approved the analyst's qualification for this method. Required for accreditation compliance.
Unique identifier for the analyte parameter. Primary key for the analyte reference catalog.

The concentration of a contaminant which, if exceeded, triggers treatment or other requirements. Primarily used for lead and copper under the Lead and Copper Rule Revisions (LCRR).
Detailed sub-classification within the analyte group (e.g., heavy metals, volatile organic compounds, disinfection byproducts, indicator organisms).
Short alphanumeric code used to identify the analyte in laboratory systems and reports. Often corresponds to LIMS internal codes or regulatory parameter codes.
High-level classification of the analyte by its chemical or biological nature (inorganic, organic, microbiological, radiological, physical, emerging contaminant).
Full descriptive name of the chemical, biological, or physical parameter being measured (e.g., Total Coliform, Lead, pH, Turbidity).
Current status of the analyte in the laboratory catalog (active, inactive, retired, pending approval, under review).

business_glossary_term: American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) Method Code
description: The ASTM International standard method code(s) for measuring this analyte, if applicable. Multiple methods may be comma-separated.
Unique numerical identifier assigned by the Chemical Abstracts Service to chemical substances. Used for unambiguous identification of chemical analytes.
Indicates whether analysis of this analyte must be performed by a state-certified or specially trained analyst.
Required container material and type for sample collection (e.g., plastic bottle, glass bottle amber, sterile bag, VOA vial).
